professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: We've been studying how democracies design their elections this semester. A handful of countries and several U.S. states have begun making voting compulsory, fining citizens who skip the ballot without a valid excuse. Some political scientists argue that mandatory turnout produces governments that reflect the whole population rather than only the most motivated, while others contend that forcing reluctant or uninformed citizens to vote distorts results and tramples on individual freedom. Should countries require all eligible citizens to vote? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think compulsory voting strengthens legitimacy. When turnout is voluntary, the loudest and most organized factions dominate, and policy drifts toward their narrow interests. Requiring everyone to participate forces candidates to address the concerns of ordinary, disengaged citizens rather than courting only the reliable base.

students.name: Marco
students.text: Coercing participation seems counterproductive to me. A reluctant voter who knows nothing about the candidates contributes noise, not insight, and may simply mark the first name listed. Genuine democratic consent should include the freedom to abstain when someone honestly feels unqualified to choose.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been examining how people form first impressions this semester. A growing number of companies now use brief recorded video interviews that an algorithm scores before any human reviews the applicant. Some researchers argue that standardizing the early screening reduces the unconscious biases interviewers carry, while others warn that these systems simply learn the prejudices buried in past hiring data and apply them at scale. Do you think employers should rely on automated video screening in hiring? Why or why not?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I'm skeptical of these tools. An algorithm trained on a company's previous hires inevitably absorbs whatever patterns produced those decisions, including discriminatory ones, and then reproduces them with a misleading veneer of objectivity. Candidates also can't tell which arbitrary cue is quietly sinking their chances.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Used carefully, automated screening can be fairer than a tired recruiter. Humans are notoriously swayed by accents, appearance, and a candidate's superficial resemblance to themselves. A consistent rubric applied identically to everyone at least removes the mood and fatigue that distort ordinary interviews.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been discussing how societies preserve their built heritage this semester. As historic neighborhoods grow more popular, many cities have imposed strict preservation rules that forbid owners from altering the exteriors of old buildings. Some historians argue these protections keep a community's character and collective memory intact, while others insist that freezing whole districts in time drives up housing costs and blocks the new construction growing populations urgently need. Should cities enforce strict historic preservation rules on old neighborhoods?

students.name: Lena
students.text: Preservation is worth the tradeoffs. A neighborhood's older architecture carries layers of history that, once demolished, can never be recovered, and that continuity gives residents a tangible sense of where they belong. Letting every block be redeveloped reduces a city to interchangeable glass towers.

students.name: Jared
students.text: I think rigid rules do real harm. When a growing city can't build, prices climb until only the wealthy can afford the very districts being protected. Preserving a handful of genuinely significant buildings makes sense, but freezing entire neighborhoods sacrifices affordability for nostalgia.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about how people build friendships as adults. A number of cities have started funding programs that deliberately pair up strangers for regular meals or activities, hoping to combat rising loneliness. Some sociologists argue that such organized matching gives isolated adults a structured path into real connection, while others believe meaningful friendship can't be engineered and that manufactured pairings feel hollow. Do you think communities should run programs that intentionally match strangers into friendships?

students.name: Claire
students.text: These programs address a genuine structural problem. Adults lose the natural meeting grounds of school and lack obvious ways to form new bonds, so loneliness becomes self-reinforcing. A program doesn't fabricate friendship; it simply creates the repeated, low-pressure contact from which real closeness can eventually grow.

students.name: Tom
students.text: I doubt friendship survives being scheduled. The bonds that last usually emerge from shared work or genuine common interest, not from being assigned a partner by a city office. Pairing strangers may ease isolation briefly, yet the arrangement risks feeling like an obligation rather than a connection.
